items.hook: Eric Garner is killed by police officer Daniel Pantaleo in New York City, after the latter put him in a prohibited chokehold while arresting him.
items.body: On July 17, 2014, Eric Garner, an 43-year-old African American man, was killed in the New York City borough of Staten Island by Daniel Pantaleo, a New York City Police Department (NYPD) officer, after putting him in a prohibited chokehold while arresting him. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: ‘A sublime, breezy confection’: writers on their 2026 songs of the summer
items.body: The annual rundown of Guardian writers picking their most played tracks of the season goes from club-ready pop to sunny tech house

items.hook: King Mohammed Zahir Shah of Afghanistan, while having surgery in Italy, is deposed by his cousin Mohammed Daoud Khan.
items.body: Mohammad Zahir Shah was the last king of Afghanistan, reigning from 8 November 1933 until he was deposed on 17 July 1973. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Donna Lee Butterworth was an American actress and singer, best known for starring opposite Elvis Presley in the 1966 musical comedy Paradise, Hawaiian Style, when she was 10 years old.
items.body: Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: Brazil win their fourth World Cup title, defeating Italy 3–2 on penalties.
items.body: The Brazil national football team, nicknamed Canarinho, represents Brazil in men's international football and is administered by the Brazilian Football Confederation, the governing body of football in Brazil. The world is stranger than it lets on.

items.hook: Henry Frédéric Chéron was a French lawyer and politician who became active in local politics in the Calvados department of Normandy while still a young man, and always maintained his roots in Normandy.
items.body: He was elected to the Chamber of Deputies and then to the Senate, and held various ministerial posts between 1913 and 1934. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: Ian Howard Marshall was a Scottish New Testament scholar.
items.body: He was Professor of New Testament Exegesis at the University of Aberdeen, Scotland. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Fleabag at 10: did Phoebe Waller-Bridge usher in a wave of female-fronted series – or straitjacket them?
items.body:
The confessional classic opened the floodgates for a generation of brilliant female showrunners. But as risk-averse streamers tighten their purse strings, is the industry forcing women’s stories back into a box?

Ten years ago, Phoe

items.hook: He worked for many companies throughout his career including DC Comics, Timely Comics, Marvel Comics, Fawcett Comics, David McKay Publications, and King Features Syndicate.
items.body: George "Jimmy" Orlando Thompson was a Canadian comic book artist during the Golden Age of Comic Books. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Hits don’t lie! Shakira’s 20 best songs, from World Cup anthems to megastar duets – ranked!
items.body:
As the Colombian pop supremo prepares to perform at Sunday’s final, we rate her greatest work, including gossipy takedowns and lycanthropic lyrics

Of Shakira’s World Cup anthems, it’s the joyfully ludicrous Waka Waka from the 2010

items.hook: Movistar Plus will support 26 Spanish series a year - Production / Funding - Spain
items.body: The Spanish platform is expanding its investment and commitment to drama by doubling the number of titles and introducing new collaboration models

items.hook: In November 2019, American actress Dakota Johnson appeared on The Ellen DeGeneres Show.
items.body: During her interview with American comedian Ellen DeGeneres, the host asked Johnson about not receiving an invitation to Johnson's then-recent 30th birthday party. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Fantasia shines a spotlight on European genre cinema with a wealth of premieres - Fantasia 2026
items.body: The new films by Agnieszka Smoczyńska and Kasper Kalle lead the European contingent at the Canadian festival

items.hook: A structural failure leads to the collapse of a walkway at the Hyatt Regency in Kansas City, Missouri, killing 114 people and injuring more than 200.
items.body: On July 17, 1981, two overhead walkways in the Hyatt Regency Hotel in Kansas City, Missouri, collapsed, killing 114 people and injuring 216. Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: Bengt Gosta Karlsson Bermsjo is a professor of social anthropology at Stockholm University.
items.body: His research engages mainly with Indigenous people in Northeast India and political ecology. Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: Ancient Egyptian princesses buried with weapons may have been fighters
items.body: The mummified daughters of pharaohs who lived thousands of years ago have bone changes that suggest they fought with bows and daggers

items.hook: The 7.7 Mw Pangandaran tsunami earthquake severely affects the Indonesian island of Java, killing 668 people, and leaving more than 9,000 injured.
items.body: An earthquake occurred on July 17, 2006, at 15:19:27 local time along a subduction zone off the coast of west and central Java, a large and densely populated island in the Indonesian archipelago. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: Norma Holzman Farber was an American children's book writer and poet.
items.body: The Poetry Society of America presents the Norma Farber First Book Award, which is awarded for a first book of original poetry written by an American. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: Mathieu Amalric's Autobiography of an Octopus now in post-production - Production / Funding - France/Belgium
items.body: Christèle Tual toplines the filmmaker’s new feature, produced by Les Films du Poisson and sold by Pyramide International, which is an adaptation of Vinciane Despret’s novel

items.hook: Members of the French National Guard under the command of General Lafayette open fire on a crowd of radical Jacobins at the Champ de Mars, Paris, during the French Revolution, killing scores of people.
items.body: The National Guard is a French military, gendarmerie, and police reserve force, active in its current form since 2016 but originally founded in 1789 during the French Revolution. One of those things you can't un-know.
